<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- The memory-* document cluster (architecture, improvements, OS behavior, risks) suggests active investment in the AI memory subsystem design — these should stay in sync with hench implementation.
- Documents like prd-steward-vision.md and level-system-reference.md indicate evolving domain language; ensure terminology in docs matches the actual code (e.g., 'level' vs. actual field names in prd.json).
- Perfect cohesion and zero coupling are expected for a pure documentation zone.
- Having AI-consumption docs (llms.txt-style) alongside human design docs in one zone is appropriate — both serve the same architectural knowledge-sharing purpose.
- The 2026-03-03 dated refresh-orchestration-memory-analysis.md suggests periodic architectural review sessions; consider a naming convention that makes the intent (decision record vs. analysis) explicit.
- Like prd-runtime-state, this zone's cohesion derives from directory co-location (docs/), not import relationships — both data and documentation zones are structurally opaque to the import graph topology.
- The docs/ zone and .rex/ zone share the same structural property (cohesion=1, coupling=0, no import edges) despite serving different roles — this suggests a potential class of 'content zones' that sourcevision could distinguish from 'code zones' in its output.
- Documentation and runtime-state zones form a 'content zone' class: import-edge-free clusters where cohesion reflects filesystem colocation rather than architectural coupling — distinct from code zones where cohesion is earned through import topology.
- Only one document uses an ISO date prefix (2026-03-03-refresh-orchestration-memory-analysis.md); all other docs use purely descriptive names. This inconsistency makes it unclear whether the date prefix signals a decision-record archetype or is incidental — readers cannot reliably distinguish time-stamped analysis artifacts from evergreen reference documents.
- Establish an explicit naming convention distinguishing evergreen reference docs (e.g. memory-architecture.md) from dated analysis artifacts (e.g. 2026-03-03-*.md). Without a consistent pattern, contributors cannot determine whether a document is stable reference material or a point-in-time snapshot.

</insights>
